cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking what advice Wesla Whitfield would most likely give to other disabled people based on the passage. Let me go through the text again to make sure I understand her perspective. The passage talks about Wesla's life. She was a jazz singer who became paralyzed from the waist down after a shooting when she was 29. Initially, she struggled with depression and even tried to take her own life. But she eventually came to terms with her situation. She emphasized that the only thing she lost was the ability to walk, but she still had her mind, which she considers her most valuable asset. She points to her head and says, "Everything important is in here. The only real disability in life is losing your mind." She also mentions that when she feels frustrated, especially when others are dancing and she can't, she removes herself to focus on what she can do. So, the key points here are that she focuses on what she can do rather than what she's lost. She values her mind and her ability to think and create. She's shifted her focus from the loss to her remaining strengths. The advice she would give would probably align with this mindset. Looking at the options: A) Ignore what you have lost and make the best use of what you have. B) Be modest and hard-working to earn respect
